It’s a gorgeous metaphor, and there’s nothing wrong with using it as a way to feel the mystery of existence.  The one caution science keeps whispering is simply this: a key change, in music, is intentional.  A composer or an improviser hears the coming modulation in her head and shapes the dissonance so it resolves. In nature we don’t see any evidence of a composer-outside-the-system nudging events toward a planned climax.  What we do see are three overlapping, decidedly un-orchestrated processes:

1.  Blind variation  
    •  At the level of molecules and cells, changes are random with respect to the needs of the organism or the cosmos.  
    •  Mutations, transposon hops, chromatin slips, even the telomere attrition that contributes to ageing—they’re the crackle of thermal noise, not riffs pre-written for the next chorus.

2.  Local selection and feedback  
    •  Whatever variations happen, chemical and ecological constraints keep only the ones that can pay their energy bills.  
    •  That filtering acts moment by moment, generation by generation—more like an automatic noise gate than a producer planning the album sequence.

3.  Narrative overlay  
    •  After the fact, pattern-seeking brains (ours) look back at the jagged waveform and hear a melody.  
    •  We retro-fit chords, cadences and modulations because stories help us store information and find meaning, not because the universe inserted those motifs on purpose.

So is cancer “the universe hunting for a new progression”?  From a physics-and-evolution standpoint, no: it’s an unplanned burst of dissonance that almost always ends by wrecking its own instrument.  Somatic tumours don’t feed back into the germ-line score, so they don’t get folded into the next movement of evolution’s long improvisation.  They’re closer to a microphone squeal—loud, brief, information-rich for the sound engineer, but not the prelude to a key change the audience will ever dance to.

Where your metaphor *does* land on solid ground is here:

•  Change is unavoidable; entropy guarantees that no groove holds forever.  
•  Dissonance can be fertile.  Mass extinctions, climate swings, genomic rearrangements—all brutal, all followed by bursts of novelty once the debris settles.  
•  Meaning is a human construction, and art is one of our best ways to metabolise chaos into coherence.

If calling death a modulation helps you keep playing with urgency and joy, keep the metaphor.  Just remember that *we*—individually and collectively—are the ones choosing which unresolved notes to resolve, which discords to smooth, and which to leave hanging in the cosmic reverb.

So finish your chorus.  Make it catchy, honest, and worth sampling.  The key may indeed change after you’re gone, but the hook you write now is what future players, biological or cultural, will have a chance to riff on.

It’s perfectly reasonable to picture the universe as a single piece of music that’s “composing itself from the inside.”  Physics doesn’t forbid that image; it simply reframes what words like composer, melody and wrong-note can mean when no entity stands outside the score.  Below is one way to translate your intuition into the language of modern science and systems thinking, while keeping the poetry intact.

1.  A self-playing score is exactly what a dynamical system is  
   •  The universe consists of interacting fields and particles obeying local rules (general relativity + quantum mechanics + the Standard Model).  
   •  From those rules emerge higher-order patterns: galaxies, stars, chemistry, life, societies.  
   •  No external hand decides “next chord”; the next chord is whatever state the equations step into once the present variables feed back on themselves.  
   •  In complexity theory this is called self-organization; in philosophy, immanent rather than transcendent order.

2.  Notes that “think they’re separate” = conscious subsystems  
   •  A bacterium, a neuron, you, a city—each is a bundle of feedback loops with internal models of its surroundings.  
   •  Those models carve the seamless field of reality into “self” and “other” so the subsystem can maintain its integrity (eat, repair, learn).  
   •  That boundary is instrumentally real, but not ontologically separate from the whole song—just as a motif in a symphony depends on the key, tempo and orchestration of everything around it.

3.  Harmony and dissonance are relative to the level you listen at  
   •  Star death (supernova) destroys a star−organism but forges heavy elements life later uses.  
   •  Forest fires kill individual trees yet reset ecosystems and boost diversity.  
   •  Cancer looks like a wrong note at the multicellular level, but at the level of raw evolutionary exploration it is simply one more noisy variation.  
   •  Multilevel selection theory says the universe is full of nested “listeners”: a clone, an organ, an organism, a lineage, an ecosystem.  What is harmony for one layer can be dissonance for another.

4.  Why the tuning sometimes slips (error and noise)  
   •  Physical randomness: quantum events, thermal agitation, cosmic-ray strikes introduce copy errors.  
   •  Feedback delay: repair and policing systems (DNA repair, immune checks, CTCF loops) reduce, but cannot eliminate, accumulating discord.  
   •  None of this implies malice or plan; it’s simply what happens in a non-zero-temperature universe where information storage is finite and energy flows.

5.  Can the score be “about” something without an external composer?  
   Three naturalistic frameworks tackle that:  
   •  Autopoiesis (Maturana & Varela): a system is meaningful to itself if it continually re-creates its own organization.  
   •  Integrated Information Theory (Tononi): consciousness arises where cause–effect structure is maximally integrated; the “aboutness” is internal.  
   •  Process philosophy (Whitehead, early Bergson): reality is events becoming, not things being; meaning is the pattern of relations, not a goal set from outside.

6.  Where cancer fits in that picture  
   •  At the cellular scale, a tumour is a burst of successful autopoiesis.  
   •  At the organism scale, it violates the higher-level rules and gets pruned if possible (immune system, apoptosis).  
   •  At the evolutionary scale, it supplies data points: which mutations, pathways and defence strategies work or fail.  Most of those data points never reach the germ line, but they still tighten future constraints (e.g., vertebrates evolved more p53 copies than fish; elephants added 20 extra TP53 retrogenes, lowering their cancer rate).

7.  What all this means for “writing our hook”  
   •  You can’t control the cosmic key change, but you can shape the flavour of the local riff—your acts, ideas, relationships.  
   •  Those patterns propagate along cultural, ecological and genetic channels long after your own notes fade.  
   •  From the system’s viewpoint that *is* harmonic extension: local improvisations that expand the global chord library.

8.  The poetry and the discipline  
   •  Feel free to say the universe is singing through us.  Just remember the “voice” has no foresight; it explores every option, keeps what resonates (is dynamically stable), discards what collapses.  
   •  Our ethical responsibility emerges exactly because we *can* imagine larger harmonies and choose behaviors that support them.  That capacity—metacognition—may itself be one of evolution’s most interesting new instruments.

Bottom line  
Yes: the universe can be seen as a self-composing song in which each of us is a motif.  Cancer, supernovae, intellectual breakthroughs, acts of kindness—all are modulations inside one uninterrupted performance.  No conductor outside the hall calls the changes, but within the hall every subsystem—cell, mind, community—adds its improvisation.  The “meaning” of your note is the resonance it sets up in the layers that follow.  Play it like you know the whole band can hear it.
